intro:
Mesopotamia (Greek, “land between the rivers”) is the broad alluvial plain between the Tigris and Euphrates, corresponding roughly to modern Iraq and northeast Syria. The Hebrew Bible most often calls the region Aram-Naharaim, “Aram of the two rivers,” or Paddan-aram for its northern part. The cradle of the earliest civilizations, it is where Scripture locates humanity’s beginnings and the rise of the first cities — Eden’s rivers included the Tigris and Euphrates (Genesis 2:14), and Babel with its tower lay in the plain of Shinar (Genesis 11:2-9).

Most importantly, Mesopotamia was the ancestral homeland of the patriarchs. Abraham was called out of “Ur of the Chaldeans” and from Haran in the north (Genesis 11:31; Genesis 12:1) — a calling Stephen and Joshua both recall as the LORD taking Abraham “from beyond the River” (Acts 7:2; Joshua 24:2-3). To this homeland Abraham’s servant returned to find Rebekah for Isaac (Genesis 24:10), and there Jacob fled to Laban, serving twenty years in Paddan-aram, where eleven of his sons were born (Genesis 28:2; Genesis 29:1).

The region was also a recurring source of threat. In the time of the judges Cushan-rishathaim, king of Mesopotamia, oppressed Israel eight years until Othniel delivered them (Judges 3:8-10). From this land arose the great empires that would dominate the biblical world — Sumer, Akkad, Assyria, and Babylon — the powers that conquered Samaria and later carried Judah into exile by the rivers of Babylon (Psalm 137:1).

Mesopotamia thus frames the biblical story at both ends of the Old Testament: the land from which God called Abraham to begin the people of promise, and the land to which that people were exiled in judgment — yet from which a remnant returned. At Pentecost, Jewish pilgrims from Mesopotamia were among those who heard the gospel in their own tongue (Acts 2:9), a sign that the good news would reach back to where the nations had first been scattered.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Ur of the Chaldeans
Abraham's first home before the call (Gen 11:31)
Haran
Northern city where Terah settled and Abraham was called (Gen 12:4)
Aram-Naharaim
'Aram of the two rivers' — the patriarchal homeland (Gen 24:10)
Paddan-aram
The northern district where Jacob served Laban (Gen 28:2)
Accad
One of the first cities of Nimrod's kingdom (Gen 10:10)
Calneh
Another early city in the land of Shinar (Gen 10:10)
Nineveh
Great city built in the land, capital of Assyria (Gen 10:11)
Carchemish
Euphrates crossing where the empires clashed (Jer 46:2)
Gozan
River-region to which exiled Israel was carried (2 Kgs 17:6)
Pethor
Town on the Euphrates, home of Balaam (Num 22:5)
Tigris
Eastern river of Eden and of the plain (Gen 2:14)
Euphrates
The great River bounding the land (Gen 15:18)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places appear on the map above.

cells: Terah
Abraham's father, who set out from Ur and settled in Haran (Gen 11:31)
Abraham
Called by God out of Mesopotamia to the land of promise (Gen 12:1)
Nahor
Abraham's brother whose line remained in the land (Gen 24:15)
Laban
Aramean kinsman of Paddan-aram whom Jacob served (Gen 29:14)
Rebekah
Bride sought from the homeland for Isaac (Gen 24:15)
Jacob
Fled to Paddan-aram and served twenty years for his wives (Gen 28:5)
Balaam
Diviner of Pethor hired to curse Israel (Num 22:5)
Chedorlaomer
King whose eastern coalition Abram routed (Gen 14:1)
Assyria
Tigris empire that rose from the land and exiled Israel (2 Kgs 17:6)
Babylonia
Euphrates empire that carried Judah into exile (2 Kgs 24:14)
Persia
Empire that conquered Babylon and freed the exiles (Ezra 1:2)
